title: Earnings Management via Not-Wholly-Owned Subsidiaries
abstract: We investigate an unexplored mechanism of earnings management: income shifting from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to help the parent company avoid losses at the expense of subsidiaries. Consolidated net income attributable to the parent company (i.e., net income) increases through this mechanism, as the parent company enjoys the full amount of the shifted earnings rather than sharing them with minority investors. We design an empirical model to directly estimate the amount of income shifted from subsidiaries to parent firms. Employing this measure, we find that firms opportunistically decrease earnings of their not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to manage net income upward to avoid losses. The results are stronger for firms with high noncontrolling ownership, firms with large subsidiaries, firms with strong influence over not-wholly-owned subsidiaries, and firms with a high level of related-party transactions. Our results are robust to alternative research designs, including controls for within-firm variations, alternative earnings thresholds, propensity score matching, and entropy balancing techniques. Our mechanism of earnings management is generalizable to other earnings management scenarios, such as share pledging.

author.Mei Luo: School of Economics and Management, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China;
author.Frank Zhang: School of Management, Yale University, New Haven, Connecticut 06520;
author.Xinyi Zhang: School of Business, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510275, China

article.1. Introduction: In this paper, we examine income shifting from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company to avoid losses, an unexplored mechanism for earnings management that contrasts with accruals and real earnings management dominating the literature (Jones 1991,Roychowdhury 2006). Because lower income in not-wholly-owned subsidiaries is partially borne by minority investors, shifting some of their income to the parent company should increase the parent company’s reported earnings. If our prediction holds true, our findings not only contribute to the earnings management literature but also carry broad implications for corporate governance and related fields, as such income shifting may hurt the interests of minority investors in subsidiaries. The logic behind this earnings management mechanism is straightforward. In cases where a parent firm has subsidiaries that are not wholly owned, net income attributable to the parent company (hereafter net income,NI) is calculated as consolidated net income (CNI), an earnings measure for the entire consolidated entity, minus noncontrolling interests in subsidiary earnings (NON)—that is,NI=CNI−NON. If the company successfully shifts one dollar of earnings from a subsidiary to the parent firm, the resultingNIwill be higher compared with leaving that dollar in the subsidiary, as the parent company gains the full amount of earnings rather than only a portion of the subsidiary earnings. We draw on a rich data set from China that provides valuable insights into income shifting from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company. In China, a firm must not only disclose a list of all its subsidiaries but also reveal its ownership in subsidiaries, each subsidiary’s industry membership, common executives between the parent and subsidiaries, and the key financial measures of significant subsidiaries.1All these data are particularly useful for our tests, but the limited data availability confines our study to China. Nonetheless, the underlying idea is general: firms can shift income from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company to boost reported earnings. Not-wholly-owned subsidiaries are a common organizational form around the world, especially in Asia (Claessens et al. 2000) and Europe (Faccio and Lang 2002), suggesting broad implications of our findings. In a spirit similar to the traditional earnings management models byJones (1991),Dechow and Dichev (2002),Roychowdhury (2006), andMcVay (2006), we develop a model for noncontrolling interests in subsidiary earnings (NON) based on their economic determinants. The residual derived from our model represents abnormalNON, which can be used to directly estimate the amount of income shifted from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company. Lower abnormalNONimplies more income shifting away from subsidiaries. We show that firms tend to shift income from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent firm to manage net income slightly above the zero threshold, a crucial earnings target in the Chinese capital market. Our story entails several testable implications for cross-sectional variation in income shifting. First, the benefit of income shifting increases with the level of noncontrolling ownership in subsidiaries, suggesting stronger results for firms with a higher percentage of noncontrolling ownership in their subsidiaries. Second, these benefits should increase with the relative size of not-wholly-owned subsidiaries, implying more pronounced results for firms with larger subsidiaries. Third, the facilitation of income shifting is enhanced when common executives exist between the parent and not-wholly-owned subsidiaries. Finally, related-party transactions serve as a mechanism to shift income from subsidiaries to the parent, suggesting stronger results for firms engaged in larger related-party transactions. Empirical results from cross-sectional tests substantiate all these predictions, further lending support to our earnings management story. We additionally conduct a battery of robustness checks and falsification tests. We find that our results are robust to alternative model specifications to estimate abnormalNON, to alternative cutoffs for small profits, to the specification of within-firm variation, to alternative research designs including propensity score matching (PSM) and entropy balancing techniques, and to regressions that include controls for an indicator of state-owned enterprises and for the percentage of state ownership. In parallel, we conduct various falsification tests to confirm that our results do not hold in such tests. Specifically, the results do not hold when we use the year before or the year after the small-profit year as a falsification test. The results likewise do not hold for pseudo earnings targets, randomly assigned pseudo small-profit firms, or small-loss firms. Notably, the results on a parent firm’s influence over subsidiaries do not hold for wholly owned subsidiaries. Overall, we find robust results across different specifications and alternative research designs, coupled with consistent insignificance in various falsification tests. Lastly, our newly documented earnings management strategy is not confined to loss avoidance, but is applicable to various scenarios where firms have strong incentives to report higher earnings. To illustrate this versatility, we examine the specific situation when a firm’s controlling shareholders pledge their company shares as collateral for personal loans. The rationale behind this investigation is rooted in the scenario’s potential threat: a decline in stock price could trigger the liquidation of pledged company shares, resulting in substantial losses for controlling shareholders. Consistent with our prediction, we find that abnormalNONis much lower when controlling shareholders pledge their company shares, suggesting that these firms shifted income from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company to report higher earnings. Our paper is related toThomas et al. (2004), who deserve credit for bringing up the idea of managing earnings via not-wholly-owned subsidiaries, a logic that we follow in our paper. However, they examine a different research question—whether the parent firm’s earnings are more managed than consolidated earnings. Many countries, including the United States, do not report parent-level financial statements so that firms do not have incentives to manage parent-level income. Furthermore, the avenues for managing parent-level income through transactions with affiliates are more extensive than those for managing consolidated income with subsidiaries. As highlighted byThomas et al. (2004), firms aiming to boost the parent’s earnings can shift income from both wholly owned and not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company.2Wholly owned subsidiaries are more potent than not-wholly-owned subsidiaries in the setting ofThomas et al. (2004), simply because consolidated earnings encompass 100% of those subsidiaries’ earnings, compared with less than 100% for not-wholly-owned subsidiaries. In contrast, wholly owned subsidiaries do not work in our setting because their income is entirely shared by the parent company, eliminating any incentives for income shifting. Indeed, we find no result when using wholly owned subsidiaries as a falsification test. Our paper makes several contributions to the literature. First, our paper is the first to empirically examine the idea of earnings management via not-wholly-owned subsidiaries. We demonstrate that companies shift earnings from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company to increase net income (i.e., consolidated earnings attributable to the parent firm). This novel earnings management technique complements traditional accruals and real earnings management. Because not-wholly-owned subsidiaries are a common organizational form, shifting income from them to parent firms could be a widespread business practice around the world. Second, we present an empirical model of income shifting from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company, a valuable tool for future researchers. This model is based on subsidiary-level characteristics, a feature that is distinct from existing earnings management models. Finally, we document an unexplored form of tunneling: tunnelingwithinthe listed firm (i.e., from the not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company), as opposed to tunnelingout ofthe listed firm (i.e., from the listed firm to the controlling shareholder), which the literature has focused on (e.g.,Bertrand et al. 2002,Jiang et al. 2010). The parties hurt in the traditional tunneling setting are minority shareholders of a listed company, whereas the parties hurt in our setting are minority shareholders of subsidiaries of the listed company. As virtually all subsidiaries worldwide are nonlisted companies, it is conceivably harder for subsidiaries’ minority shareholders to discipline the controlling shareholder because of the lack of robust corporate governance mechanisms like independent directors, analysts, and auditors. Furthermore, the shares of nonlisted companies are typically highly illiquid, making it difficult for those minority shareholders to exercise their influence by voting with their feet and divesting from the company. We organize the rest of the paper as follows.Section 2reviews the relevant literature, discusses ways to shift income from subsidiaries to the parent company, and develops our empirical approaches.Section 3describes our sample and descriptive statistics.Section 4presents our main empirical results.Section 5discusses additional tests, andSection 6concludes.
article.2. Prior Research and the Concept of Inc: 2.1. Prior Literature This paper relates to two strands of literature. The first strand is the extensive research on earnings management, particularly the means of earnings management (see reviews bySchipper 1989,Healy and Wahlen 1999,Dechow and Skinner 2000,Fields et al. 2001, andDechow et al. 2010). This literature shows that firms use accruals and real business activities to manage earnings (Jones 1991,Roychowdhury 2006). Prior studies typically construct measures of earnings management based on financial numbers from the entire consolidated entity, a line-by-line aggregation of the parent company and its subsidiaries’ respective financial statements (after consolidation adjustments). Discretionary accruals, a popular proxy for earnings management, are a good example. Variables used in theJones (1991)or modified Jones model to calculate discretionary accruals, such as total accruals, sales revenues, and Property, Plant, and Equipment, measure the performance of the entire parent firm and 100% of its subsidiaries. However, net income—the usual earnings management target—represents only the amount benefiting the parent company. Therefore, these earnings management proxies implicitly assume that the amount benefiting the parent company (NI) and the noncontrolling interests in subsidiary earnings (NON) exhibit the same characteristics after firms exercise earnings management through accruals. Some prior work specifically examines earnings management through subsidiaries. Those studies generally focus on increasing subsidiaries’ earnings to increase the consolidated net income comprising the parent’s and subsidiaries’ earnings. For example,Dyreng et al. (2012)find that U.S. multinational corporations with material subsidiaries in weak-rule-of-law countries, or in tax havens, have more discretionary accruals and that earnings management is concentrated in foreign income, suggesting earnings management through foreign subsidiaries.Bonacchi et al. (2018)find that Italian nonlisted subsidiaries engage in accruals and real earnings management to enable their listed parents to meet or beat benchmarks.Beuselinck et al. (2019)confirm this phenomenon and further document that the influence of headquarters on subsidiaries’ earnings management increases with the degree of subsidiary integration and the extent of earnings management opportunities. None of these prior studies examine income shifting between the parent and its subsidiaries: they all assume that to manage the parent’s earnings upward, subsidiaries’ earnings need to go up. In contrast, in our paper subsidiaries’ earnings must go down to manage the parent’s earnings upward. Studies on the earnings management role of noncontrolling interests in subsidiary earnings are relatively scant. When a parent firm has not-wholly-owned subsidiaries, consolidated net income attributable to the parent company (NI) will share only a portion of subsidiary earnings, leaving the rest to noncontrolling interests in subsidiary earnings (NON). Shifting one dollar of earnings from a subsidiary to the parent firm will allow the parent company’s net income to include the full dollar, rather than only a fraction. The parent company can benefit more from this income shifting if noncontrolling interests constitute a greater percentage of a subsidiary’s ownership.Thomas et al. (2004)bring up this idea of managing earnings via not-wholly-owned subsidiaries, but they do not examine this research question. By utilizing detailed subsidiary data, we are the first to empirically examine earnings management via not-wholly-owned subsidiaries and to present an empirical model of such earnings management, providing a valuable tool for future researchers. The second relevant strand of literature engages tunneling within business groups. A business group is a set of firms that are legally independent but are bound together by formal and informal ties and are accustomed to taking coordinated actions (Khanna and Rivkin 2001).3Tunneling within a business group refers to expropriating wealth from some shareholders to other shareholders (e.g.,Johnson et al. 2000,Bae et al. 2002,Baek et al. 2006,Jiang et al. 2010). For example,Bertrand et al. (2002)show that owners of business groups tunnel resources from firms where they have low cash-flow rights to firms where they have high cash-flow rights, especially in countries with weak investor protection.Jiang et al. (2010)document how controlling shareholders siphon funds from publicly traded companies through intercompany loans.Liu and Lu (2007)andAharony et al. (2010)show that a controlling shareholder can help a listed firm manage earnings in initial public offerings (IPOs) and seasoned equity offerings (SEOs), so that she can tunnel raised capital from the listed firm to herself (i.e., a company that she controls but is not publicly traded). Virtually all prior studies focus on tunnelingout ofa publicly traded company, going from the listed firm to the controlling shareholder. In contrast, we examine tunnelingwithinthe listed firm, going from the not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company.4Although this form of tunneling is unexplored in the literature, it could be significant because a subsidiary’s minority shareholders suffer from a severe agency problem when they do not have many corporate governance mechanisms (e.g., independent directors, analysts, auditors) in place. More importantly, as noted above, the nonlisted companies’ illiquid shares often prevent minority shareholders from simply divesting from the company to signal their disapproval. 2.2. Ways to Shift Income from a Subsidiary to the Parent Company The consolidation rules in China closely follow international financial reporting standards: firms must report noncontrolling interests in a subsidiary, in conformity with the requirements of International Financial Reporting Standard (IFRS) 3 (Business Combinations) and IFRS 10 (Consolidated Financial Statements), both of which are similar to accounting rules for business combinations and noncontrolling interests in the United States.5Consolidated financial statements report the activities of the parent company and all entities under its control (i.e., its subsidiaries). The consolidation process combines the account balances from the separate books of the parent and each subsidiary after adjustments and eliminations, as if all the consolidating companies were a single company. In many cases, subsidiaries are not fully owned by the parent. A parent could even consolidate a subsidiary without majority ownership, that is, less than 50% ownership, if it can determine the direction of the subsidiary’s management through contract or other means, such as holding majority directorships in the subsidiary. A noncontrolling interest is the portion of equity ownership in a subsidiary that is not attributable, directly or indirectly, to the parent company. Therefore, the noncontrolling interests of outside shareholders in the subsidiaries’ equity and net income are saliently reported on consolidated balance sheets and income statements. In the income statement, reported revenue, expenses, andCNIreflect the full activities of the parent and its subsidiaries. The earnings measure for the entire consolidated entity,CNI, is allocated between the shareholders of the parent company (NI) and noncontrolling interests (NON). The amount of income attributable to noncontrolling interests (NON) is generally based on the earnings of the noncontrolling share in each subsidiary, whereas the consolidated net income attributable to the parent company (NI) is associated with the parent’s earnings and the earnings of the controlling share in each subsidiary. The assets and liabilities of all subsidiaries and the parent company are reported on the consolidated balance sheet as a whole. To balance the balance sheet, interests of outside shareholders in the subsidiaries’ equity (noncontrolling interests) are shown in the shareholders’ equity section of the consolidated balance sheet. Economic transactions called intercompany transactions may occur between units of the same consolidated entity. Although these transactions can occur for a variety of reasons, they often transpire as a result of the normal business relationships between the units of the entity. Intercompany transactions can flow from the parent to a subsidiary (downstream transactions), from a subsidiary to the parent (upstream transactions), or between subsidiaries (lateral transactions). When companies have intercompany transactions not involving any party outside of the consolidated entity, the standards require elimination of any intercompany revenues, expenses, income, or losses when preparing the consolidated entity’s financial statements, as if the parent company and its subsidiaries were a single company with one or more branches or divisions.6Any resulting income or loss between the parent company and its subsidiaries is considered unrealized from the consolidated entity’s perspective, regardless of the direction of transaction flow or the existence of a noncontrolling interest. These transactions will generate zero earnings to consolidated net income (CNI) and to the amount of income attributable to the parent company (NI) or noncontrolling interests (NON) on the income statement. Therefore, without the involvement of outside parties, shifting earnings from subsidiaries to the parent firm through intercompany transactions would not help a company manage its earnings. When intercompany transactions involve parties outside of the consolidated entity, profits or losses arising from intercompany transactions (e.g., sale of inventory between the parent and a subsidiary) are considered realized and are recognized in consolidated financial statements.7The first example in Online Appendix 1 illustrates income shifting from a subsidiary to the parent company by increasing the internal sale price: the parent company sells a product at a higher-than-normal price to its subsidiary, which in turn sells the product at the regular price to an outside party. If the sales price (from parent to subsidiary) is $20 more than normal, the consolidated entity essentially shifts $20 from the subsidiary to the parent company. The net effect on net income attributable to the parent’s shareholders (NI) is 20 − 20*(1 −X%)—that is, $20*X%, whereX% is the percentage of noncontrolling ownership in the subsidiary. Therefore, consolidated net income attributable to the parent company will always increase if the company can shift earnings from a not-wholly-owned subsidiary to the parent firm, because the parent company can share only a portion of subsidiary earnings ($20*(1 −X%) in the earlier example) but can enjoy the full amount of its own earnings ($20 if shifted from the subsidiary to the parent). The parent company can benefit more from earnings shifting if the noncontrolling shareholders constitute a greater percentage of the subsidiary’s ownership—that is, a greaterX. Companies can shift income from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent through intercompany transactions as well as related-party transactions on a broader scale, outside of the consolidated entity.8The second approach in Online Appendix 1 provides an anecdotal example of shifting income through related-party transactions by a well-known public company in China. Both the parent company and its subsidiary sell products and services to the same related party outside the consolidated entity. The subsidiary sells products to the related party at a huge discount to help the parent company acquire new customers and boost revenues at favorable terms from the related party. The related party does not need to bear any cost. The parent company then shows strong growth in net income (NI), whereas the subsidiary reports huge losses. In sum, through intercompany transactions and related-party transactions, firms can increase consolidated net income attributable to the parent company by decreasing the earnings of not-wholly-owned subsidiaries, which in turn lowers the noncontrolling interests in subsidiaries’ earnings. Anecdotal evidence supports the relevance of our research question. During the approval process of IPOs and SEOs, both stock exchanges and the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC; the Chinese version of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)) often express concerns and issue comment letters requiring firms to explain why their noncontrolling interests in subsidiary earnings are negative when the consolidated net income attributable to the parent is positive.Appendix Bprovides three anecdotal examples of such inquiries from regulators. In this paper, we empirically investigate whether firms opportunistically shift income from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company. 2.3. Empirical Approach In a spirit similar to traditional earnings management models (e.g.,Jones 1991,McVay 2006,Roychowdhury 2006), we estimate an economic model of noncontrolling interests in subsidiary earnings (NON) and use the residual (i.e., abnormalNON) to capture the effect of income shifting, which is then linked to financial reporting incentives.9Specifically, our empirical approach includes two steps.10First, inEquation (1)we estimate a model ofNONas a function of its economic determinants. Equation (1)is an expanded time-series model ofNON, whereNONis the noncontrolling interests in subsidiary earnings scaled by the average total assets. The first three explanatory variables operate similarly to the time-series models of earnings in the literature (e.g.,Watts and Leftwich 1977,Bernard and Thomas 1989) used to estimate abnormal earnings, analogous to abnormalNONin our model. LaggedNONcaptures the persistence ofNON, which goes a long way in controlling for the economic factors affectingNON. We predict the coefficient onNONt−1to be positive. We also account for the potential asymmetry in persistence between profit and loss by including a dummy variable for a negativeNONin yeart− 1 (NEGNONt−1) and its interaction term withNONt−1. We expect the coefficients onNEGNONt−1andNONt−1×NEGNONETt−1to be negative because loss is less persistent than profit (Hayn 1995,Lawrence et al. 2018). We also include the noncontrolling interests on the consolidated balance sheet, scaled by the total assets (NCI), inEquation (1).NCIrepresents the portion of equity interest in subsidiaries that is not attributable to the parent company. Conceptually,NCIcaptures noncontrolling shareholders’ investment in subsidiaries and thus should be positively correlated withNON. Next, we include average annual industry returns across a firm’s not-wholly-owned subsidiaries (SRET) inEquation (1), which captures the subsidiaries’ industry-level information and corresponding industry-wide economic factors. We expect the coefficient onSRETto be positive because of industry-wide economic comovements. As the percentage of noncontrolling ownership mechanically affectsNON, we likewise include a firm’s average percentage of noncontrolling ownership across all not-wholly-owned subsidiaries (NON_MEAN) inEquation (1). We expect a positive coefficient onNON_MEANin the model. Finally, we include the natural logarithm of one plus the number of not-wholly-owned subsidiaries (NUMNWO) inEquation (1). In a spirit similar toNCIandNON_MEAN, we also expect the coefficient onNUMNWOto be positive. To sum up, theNONmodel is purely based on variables capturing subsidiary-level characteristics. We estimateEquation (1)by pooling regressions with industry fixed effects of a firm’s not-wholly-owned subsidiaries (SUBIND) and year fixed effects (YEAR).11Nonetheless, our results are robust to running regressions by the firm’s industry, by year, or by industry-year. The residual from estimatingEquation (1)is denoted as abnormalNON(ABNON), which is our measure of income shifted opportunistically from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company. In the second step, we regressABNONon our indicator of earnings management—small profit (SP)—and the control variables as follows: SPis a dummy variable with the value of one ifNI(i.e., consolidated net income attributable to the parent company, scaled by average total assets) is equal to or greater than zero but less than 0.5%, and zero otherwise. Essentially, firms withSP= 1 are the usual suspects that manage earnings upward to avoid losses. In China, a firm will be suspended from trading if it reports losses for three consecutive years, and further losses will trigger delisting. Similarly, a firm must be profitable for three consecutive years to qualify to issue a seasoned equity offering. This contracting role of earnings gives firms strong incentives to avoid losses (Aharony et al. 2000,Chen and Yuan 2004,Jian and Wong 2010,He et al. 2012). Whereas parent companies are publicly traded and have strong incentives to avoid losses, virtually all subsidiaries are nonlisted companies and thus have much weaker reporting incentives. Whereas theNONmodel inEquation (1)is based on subsidiary-level characteristics,Equation (2)controls for firm-level variables (CONTROLS), including the consolidated net income attributable to the parent company scaled by average total assets (NI), the market value of equity (SIZE), the book-to-market ratio (BM), leverage (LEV), the annual returns (RET), an indicator variable for loss firms (LOSS), and the firm’s industry (FIRMIND) and year fixed effects. The logic is that our main variable of interest,SP, is a firm-level variable, so we control for firm-level variables in this model. Including firm-level variables also helps address a potential mechanical relationship betweenABNONandSP. Specifically,SPis a partitioning variable based onNI. AsNONandNIshare the subsidiary earnings, any misspecification ofEquation (1)could lead to a mechanical relation betweenABNONandNI. Therefore,SPandABNONcould be mechanically correlated because of their association withNI. Controlling forNIin the model helps alleviate this issue. In subsequent sections, we conduct a variety of robustness, falsification, and cross-sectional tests to further examine and address this concern. We expect firms to shift income out of not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent firm, so we expectβ1to be negative inEquation (2).12Importantly,β1is directly linked to the amount of income shifted from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company. SupposeX% is the percentage of noncontrolling ownership in the subsidiary. If $1 is shifted from the subsidiary to the parent company,ABNONwill be reduced by $1*X%. Thus,β1fromEquation (2)divided byX% is the amount of income shifted from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company. We can extendEquation (2)to explore cross-sectional variations in earnings management, as follows: Appendix Apresents detailed variable definitions. Each year, all continuous control variables exceptSRETandRETare Winsorized at 1% and 99%. Standard errors are clustered at the firm level in these models.
article.3. Sample and Descriptive Statistics: 3.1. Sample and Data Our sample period spans from 2007 to 2022. The sample starts in 2007 because China adopted new accounting standards on January 1, 2007, including standards related to business combinations and accounting for noncontrolling interests in subsidiaries.13We obtain the data about a parent company’s ownership in its subsidiaries and the financial data from the CSMAR databases, which are available through Wharton Research Data Services. We obtain the industry classification of subsidiaries from the CNRDS database. We exclude financial firms and require firms to have a nonzero noncontrolling interest in subsidiary earnings. That is, firms must have at least one not-wholly-owned subsidiary. We further require nonmissing net income attributable to the parent company, net income to the noncontrolling interests, and other variables of interest. Our final sample consists of 31,225 firm-year observations over the 2007–2022 sample period. Panel A ofTable 1presents the sample selection process. The mean earnings attributable to noncontrolling interests in a subsidiary, scaled by average total assets (NON), are 0.2%, whereas the mean consolidated net income attributable to the parent company (NI) is 2.9%, indicating that noncontrolling interests account for 6.45% (=0.2/(2.9 + 0.2)) of the entire consolidated net income, a nonnegligible amount. The average value ofNON_MEANsuggests that noncontrolling shareholders, on average, own 31.8% of not-wholly-owned subsidiaries. In addition, an average firm has about five not-wholly-owned subsidiaries (NUMNWO), indicating the prevalence of such organizational forms.14 Panel C ofTable 1provides the Pearson and Spearman correlations. All determinant variables inEquation (1)have the expected signs of correlations withNON. Specifically,NEGNONt−1has a significantly negative correlation withNONt, whereasNCIt−1,SRETt,NON_MEANt, andNUMNWOtall have significantly positive correlations withNONt. Besides,NONthas a significantly positive correlation withNIt, suggesting that the earnings performance of a firm’s subsidiary tends to comove with the parent company, owing to economy- or industry-wide factors and their respective shares in subsidiary earnings. However,NONthas a significantly negative correlation with the dummy for small-profit firms (SPt), implying lower net income attributable to noncontrolling interests for firms that just meet or beat the zero-profit benchmark. The correlation coefficients with control variables indicate that small-profit firms have smaller market capitalization and annual stock returns but have higher book-to-market ratios and leverage. As discussed earlier, firms have strong incentives to manage earnings to avoid losses because of the contracting role of earnings in the Chinese setting (He et al. 2012,Luo et al. 2018).Figure 1presents a histogram of consolidated net income attributable to the parent company (NI) in our sample. The figure shows the frequency of firms ranging inNIfrom −30% to 30%, with an interval width of 0.5%. The distinct discontinuity around zero suggests that an unusually high number of firms have earnings meeting or slightly beating the zero-profit benchmark, confirming evidence documented in the prior earnings management literature (Burgstahler and Dichev 1997,Jiang and Wang 2008,Jian and Wong 2010). The number of firms just missing the zero-profit target is unusually low with only 145 observations, whereas the number of firms just meeting the target is extremely high with 2,063 observations. In line with the literature, we posit that firms falling into the first interval to the right of zero (NIbetween 0% and 0.5%) have strong incentives to manage earnings to avoid losses.
article.4. Main Empirical Results: 4.1. Estimation of ABNON and Validity Tests Panel A ofTable 2presents the estimation results ofEquation (1). We find that all the signs of the coefficients are consistent with our expectations, and all the variables have coefficient estimates significant at the 1% level. Specifically, noncontrolling interests in subsidiary earnings (NON) exhibit a high persistence, with a significant coefficient of 0.68 onNONt−1. Persistence declines for losses, as indicated by the significantly negative coefficients onNEGNONt−1andNONt−1×NEGNONt−1. In addition,NONincreases with the size of not-wholly-owned subsidiaries, the percentage of the noncontrolling ownership, and the number of not-wholly-owned subsidiaries, as evidenced in the significantly positive coefficients onNCIt−1,NON_MEANt, andNUMNWOt, respectively. Meanwhile,NONis positively correlated with subsidiary industry returns (SRETt), with a coefficient of 0.0012 and at-value of 4.65. The adjustedR2forEquation (1)is 47.42%, indicating a good fit. article.5. Additional Analyses: 5.1. Cross-sectional Variation by Noncontrolling Ownership in Subsidiaries The earnings management mechanism we propose posits that the entire benefit of income shifting flows to the parent company, whereas the cost is shared by noncontrolling shareholders. An important implication of this story is that the benefit to the parent company increases when noncontrolling shareholders own more of the subsidiary. DenoteX% as the percentage of noncontrolling shareholders’ ownership in the subsidiary. If the parent company shifts one dollar of earnings from the subsidiary to itself, the consolidated net income attributable to the parent company will increase by this one dollar, but at the same time decrease by its share of the subsidiary’s one-dollar earnings, $1*(1 −X%), resulting in a net benefit from income shifting of $1*X%. Therefore, the greater the ownership of noncontrolling shareholders in subsidiaries, the higher the net benefit from income shifting. A caveat is that the parent company’s ability to shift income may decrease as the ownership of noncontrolling shareholders increases. On balance, we expect our results to be more pronounced for firms with higher noncontrolling ownership. To test this implication, we estimateEquation (3)discussed inSection 2.3, whereXis defined as a dummy variable indicating the existence of a large noncontrolling ownership in subsidiaries for each firm-year, in three alternative ways. The CSRC requires firms to disclose the parent company’s ownership in each subsidiary. The noncontrolling ownership in each subsidiary is then equal to one minus the parent’s ownership percentage. For each firm-year, we calculate the average percentage of noncontrolling ownership across all not-wholly-owned subsidiaries (NON_MEAN), the maximum percentage of noncontrolling ownership among all not-wholly-owned subsidiaries (NON_MAX), and the portion of the subsidiaries whose percentage of noncontrolling ownership is equal to or greater than 49% of the total number of not-wholly-owned subsidiaries (NON_RATIO).22Thus,Xtakes the value of one ifNON_MEAN,NON_MAX, orNON_RATIOis above the industry median in a given year, and zero otherwise. We expect a negative coefficient on the variable of interestSP*X, that is,β3< 0, indicating stronger income shifting for firms with higher noncontrolling ownership. Panel A ofTable 6presents the regression results. The coefficients on the interaction term,SP*X, are significantly negative across all three columns, reaching −0.0006 (t= −2.75), −0.0007 (t= −3.19), and −0.0007 (t= −3.53) whenXis based onNON_MEAN,NON_MAX, andNON_RATIO, respectively. The sums of the coefficients onSPand the interaction term (i.e.,SP+SP*X) are also significantly negative across all three columns. These results are consistent with our prediction that the results of income shifting from subsidiaries to the parent company are stronger for firms with higher noncontrolling ownership in subsidiaries. Cross-Sectional Variation by the Relative Size of Subsidiaries Under our story that the parent company shifts income from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to itself to avoid losses, earnings management should be more effective when the size of subsidiaries relative to the parent company increases. If the subsidiaries are too small relative to the parent company, any income shifting would not materially change the parent company’s reported earnings. In this section, we empirically test this prediction. We collect relative size information from two sources in CSMAR. The first source is the parent company’s subsidiary footnotes, where firms are required to disclose key financial measures of subsidiaries when such subsidiaries (labeled “significant subsidiaries”) contribute 10% or more to the consolidated net income. The second source is the parent company’s long-term equity investment footnote, where firms voluntarily disclose the value of the parent company’s investment in investees and some investees’ financial figures. We find that assets, equity, and net income data are available for 29.3%, 46.5%, and 29.2% of not-wholly-owned subsidiaries in our sample, respectively.23 We construct three size measures of not-wholly-owned subsidiaries relative to the parent company for each firm-year. The first relative size measure is defined as the sum of total assets across a firm’s significant not-wholly-owned subsidiaries, divided by the total assets of the consolidated entity (S_ASSET). The second relative size measure is defined as the sum of shareholders’ equity across a firm’s significant not-wholly-owned subsidiaries, divided by the shareholders’ equity of the consolidated entity (S_EQUITY). The third measure is defined as the sum of net income across a firm’s significant not-wholly-owned subsidiaries, divided by the net income of the consolidated entity (S_INCOME), where the denominator is required to be positive. We lag all three relative size measures by one year to avoid potential mechanical relations between these cross-sectional measures andABNON.24We then rerunEquation (3), whereXtakes the value of one ifS_ASSET,S_EQUITY, orS_INCOMEis above the industry median in a given year and zero otherwise. We predict the results to be stronger for firms with relatively large subsidiaries, that is, a negative coefficient on the interaction term,SP*X. Panel B ofTable 6reports the results. Across all three measures, the coefficients onSP*Xare strongly negative at −0.0011 (t= −5.34), −0.0008 (t= −3.91), and −0.0014 (t= −5.97) forXbased onS_ASSET,S_EQUITY, andS_INCOME, respectively. The sums of the coefficients onSPandSP*Xare also significantly negative across all three columns. Meanwhile, the coefficients onSPare all statistically insignificant, indicating that firms with small not-wholly-owned subsidiaries do not actively engage in such income shifting. Overall, the results strongly support our prediction that the evidence of earnings management via not-wholly-owned subsidiaries increases with the size of subsidiaries relative to the parent company. 5.3. Cross-Sectional Variation by the Parent Company’s Influence over Subsidiaries Companies sometimes concurrently appoint their senior executives (e.g., CEO, Chairman, or CFO) to the board of directors or executive team of their subsidiaries. The presence of common executives can directly determine transfer pricing and facilitate transactions between subsidiaries and the parent company, or with related parties outside of the firm (both approaches are illustrated in Online Appendix 1), especially for not-wholly-owned subsidiaries that are less integrated with the parent company owing to a noncontrolling interest (Beuselinck et al. 2019). In this section, we use the appointment of common executives as a proxy for the parent company’s influence over subsidiaries, and examine whether earnings management via not-wholly-owned subsidiaries increases with the parent’s influence on subsidiaries by estimatingEquation (8). The CSRC requires Chinese firms to disclose their senior executives’ positions in all business entities, including subsidiaries. We use the dummy variableEXE_NWOto denote the appointment of a parent’s top executives to the board or senior management team of any not-wholly-owned subsidiary, but not of any wholly owned subsidiary.EXE_NWOtakes a value of one if such common management exists and zero otherwise. As shown in the first column inTable 7, the coefficient onSP*EXE_NWOis highly negative, with an estimate of −0.0010 (t= −2.08). This negative coefficient supports the idea that appointing common executives to both the parent company and its not-wholly-owned subsidiary facilitates income shifting from those subsidiaries to the parent company. Under our story, income shifting occurs only between not-wholly-owned subsidiaries and the parent company. We thus define another dummy variable,EXE_WO, which takes the value of one if a parent’s top executives are appointed to the board or senior management team of any wholly owned subsidiary but not of any not-wholly-owned subsidiary, and zero otherwise. Rather, it is the common management between the parent company and not-wholly-owned subsidiaries that helps to facilitate income shifting from subsidiaries to the parent company. 5.4. The Role of Related-Party Transactions As illustrated in Online Appendix 1, firms can shift income from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company through intercompany transactions between a parent firm and its subsidiaries, or through related-party transactions where both a parent firm and its subsidiaries do business with the same party outside of the consolidated entity. Similarly to ASC 850, China Accounting Standards for Business Enterprises (ASBE) No. 36 requires disclosure of all material related-party transactions, but any intercompany transactions that are eliminated in consolidated financial statements need not be disclosed. Because of this data limitation, we cannot design a test to track intercompany transactions, but we can design a test using related-party transactions. TheLe.comcase in Online Appendix 1 shows that the steadily growing net income for the parent firm (NI) was accompanied by increasing losses for the not-wholly-owned subsidiary firm (NON). At the same time, the value of related-party transactions increased over time, suggesting that related-party transactions served as a conduit for shifting income from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company. Using this anecdotal insight, we expect that when an outside related party cooperates with the parent company and its subsidiary, the value of related-party transactions will increase, on average, relative to the case when the outside party conducts arm’s-length transactions with only the parent company or its subsidiary. To test this idea, we constructRPT, which takes the value of one if the total value of related-party transactions labeled as “sale/purchase of products or services” in CSMAR, scaled by the average total assets, is above the industry-year median, and zero otherwise.25We then interactSPwithRPTas inEquation (11). Table 8reports the results. In line with our predictions, we find that the coefficient onSP*RPTinEquation (11)is significantly negative at the 5% level. Our results onRPTare distinct from the “prop-up” theory documented in prior literature (e.g.,Jian and Wong 2010), which suggests that the related party directly injects profits into the parent company or its subsidiaries to prop up their net income. Our results instead present the related party as a facilitator that transfers income from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent company without bearing any cost, whereas in the prop-up theory related parties bear the costs of income injection. 5.5. Another Earnings Management Scenario: Share Pledging As discussed earlier, we focus on the loss avoidance setting because Chinese firms have strong incentives to avoid losses for contractual reasons, leading to a strong discontinuity in the earnings distribution around zero.27Other than the loss avoidance scenario, there should exist other scenarios in which firms have strong incentives to report higher earnings. If our newly documented earnings management technique is valid, it should manifest in these scenarios as well. To test this idea, in this section we consider share pledging as another earnings management scenario. When the controlling shareholders pledge their company shares as collateral for personal loans, firms have strong incentives to report high earnings to support stock prices (e.g.,Dejong et al. 2020), because a stock price drop could trigger the liquidation of pledged company shares, resulting in substantial losses to the controlling shareholders (Xie and Zhang 2021).28Share pledging is a worldwide phenomenon and is particularly prevalent in China. According to Bloomberg, about 22% of China-listed firms had more than 30% of their shares pledged for personal loans in the first half of 2018.29 To examine the share pledge setting, we simply replaceSPwithPLEDGEin the original regression models, wherePLEDGEis a dummy variable with the value of one for firm-years when a controlling shareholder pledges that person’s shares for personal loans at a percentage above the industry median in a given year, and zero otherwise. Specifically, we runEquations (12)–(15) below. Xis a cross-sectional variable with the value of one if the variable it represents (NON_MEAN,NON_MAX,NON_RATIO,S_ASSET,S_EQUITY, orS_INCOME) is above the industry median in a given year, and zero otherwise. As in the loss avoidance setting, we expect the coefficient onPLEDGEto be negative inEquation (12), suggesting that firms report higher earnings by shifting income from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries when their controlling shareholders pledge their company shares as collateral for personal loans. We also expect the coefficients onPLEDGE*Xto be negative inEquation (13), suggesting more income shifting when the level of noncontrolling ownership is higher or when the relative size of not-wholly-owned subsidiaries is larger. InEquations (14)and (15), we expect the coefficients onPLEDGE*EXE_NWOandPLEDGE*RPTto be negative, analogous to the loss avoidance setting. Table 9reports the regression results. In Panel A, the coefficient onPLEDGEis significantly negative inEquation (12), consistent with our expectation. In the second column of Panel A, we put two earnings management indicators (SPandPLEDGE) in the same regression and find significantly negative coefficients on bothPLEDGEandSP, suggesting that the explanatory power is barely overlapped between these two variables. Panels B and C examine cross-sectional variation by the level of noncontrolling ownership and the relative size of the subsidiaries, respectively. The coefficient onPLEDGE*Xis significantly negative at the 1% level whenXisNON_MAX,S_ASSET,S_EQUITY, orS_INCOME. Panel D reports the cross-sectional results on the role of common executives (EXE_NWO) and related-party transactions (RPT). We do not find a significantly negative coefficient onPLEDGE*EXE_NWO, but we observe a significantly negative coefficient onPLEDGE*RPTat the 5% level. These results seem to suggest that in the case of share pledging, firms are more likely to shift earnings via related-party transactions outside the firm rather than via intercompany transactions. article.6. Conclusions: In this paper, we explore how firms leverage noncontrolling ownership in subsidiaries to strategically manage earnings slightly above zero, a common earnings target for Chinese firms. As the parent company must share earnings from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries with noncontrolling shareholders, the parent has incentives to shift earnings from a subsidiary to itself to enjoy the full amount of shifted earnings. We examine whether firms opportunistically decrease earnings of not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to increase the consolidated net income attributable to the parent company. We find that firms tend to shift income from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent to manage consolidated net income upward and avoid losses. These results are robust to alternative model specifications to estimate abnormalNON, alternative cutoffs of small-profit firms, controls for within-firm variations, and alternative research designs including propensity score matching and entropy balancing techniques. Such income shifting does not exist in various falsification tests, including analyzing the year before or after the small-profit year, pseudo earnings targets, and randomly assigned pseudo small-profit firms. We also find that the results vary cross-sectionally in a predictable way. Firms shift more income from not-wholly-owned subsidiaries to the parent when the level of noncontrolling ownership is higher, when the relative size of subsidiaries is larger, when the parent’s influence over subsidiaries is stronger, and when the level of related-party transactions is higher. Finally, our story of income shifting via not-wholly-owned subsidiaries is generalizable, as we find similar evidence of earnings management in the setting of share pledging.
